title: When Healing Spaces Replicate Power Structures: The Body’s Silent Protest
dek: *When the practitioner's office feels like your father's study, when you find yourself performing compliance instead of speaking truth, when your neck tightens at the phrase "just trust the process" — your body is tracking patterns of dominance that your mind might dismiss as professional authority. This is the silent protest of tissues that remember what happens when power goes unquestioned.*
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Your body may tense, shut down, or flare with symptoms in therapy or healing spaces that unconsciously mirror old power dynamics. This physiological resistance isn't sabotage but intelligent self-protection signaling that the relational container itself needs examination. True healing requires spaces where anger, dissent, and boundary-setting are welcomed as data, not pathologized as resistance.

Have you noticed how your throat closes when you disagree with your therapist, even when the disagreement is small and reasonable? That constriction isn't random. It's your vagus nerve downshifting into an old familiar gear, the one labeled "don't challenge authority or you'll lose access to care." The healing space was supposed to be different. You chose someone with good reviews, appropriate credentials, a soothing voice. But three months in, you're performing wellness instead of experiencing it. You leave sessions feeling vaguely uneasy, a tightness between your shoulder blades you can't name. In our experience, this discomfort often points to something structural rather than personal: the therapeutic relationship has quietly replicated a power dynamic your body knows intimately from childhood, from institutions, from every place you learned that your survival depended on not making waves. ## The Body as Institutional Memory Your tissues hold a different kind of record than your conscious memory. While you might rationally understand that your acupuncturist is not your dismissive parent, your fascia doesn't make such neat distinctions. It responds to patterns: the tone of voice that brooks no questioning, the treatment plan presented as non-negotiable, the subtle implication that your hesitation is the problem. Karla McLaren's work on emotional flow emphasizes that anger, in particular, carries boundary-setting information. When anger arises in response to a practitioner's approach and you immediately suppress it as "inappropriate" or "ungrateful," you're repeating a somatic pattern that likely predates this relationship by decades. The anger isn't irrational. It's your system's way of flagging: this interaction shape feels dangerous. Over the years I've seen clients develop inexplicable physical symptoms - migraines before therapy appointments, digestive upset after bodywork sessions, insomnia following spiritual direction meetings - that resolve when they switch to practitioners who explicitly share power. The symptoms weren't psychosomatic in the dismissive sense. They were the body's eloquent refusal to participate in its own subordination, even in spaces ostensibly devoted to healing. The cardiovascular system, particularly, responds to suppressed anger and perceived powerlessness. PubMed research on anger expression and cardiovascular load demonstrates measurable physiological costs when we habitually inhibit anger in hierarchical relationships. Your elevated blood pressure in the waiting room isn't anxiety about getting better. It's your body's protest against reentering a relational structure where your "no" doesn't count. ## The Architecture of Assumed Authority Notice the physical setup of most healing spaces. The practitioner's chair is positioned as command center. You, the client, occupy the vulnerable seat - literally lower, facing the light, your back to the door. These aren't accidental choices. They're architectural translations of an assumed hierarchy: one person holds knowledge, the other receives it. One person is competent, the other is broken and seeking repair. This spatial grammar speaks directly to your nervous system before a single word is exchanged. If you grew up in environments where adults' comfort and authority were centered while your needs were secondary, this setup will feel simultaneously familiar and subtly threatening. Your shoulders will round forward. Your breath will shallow. You'll smile more than the moment warrants. Some practitioners recognize this dynamic and intentionally disrupt it. They offer seating choices. They position themselves at your level. They name the power differential explicitly and invite you to call it out when it feels problematic. But many don't, because they've never examined how the structure itself does harm, how the container can undermine the content of even the most compassionate therapeutic work. Beverly Engel's research on shame and power highlights how survivors of abuse often unconsciously seek out helping relationships that replicate familiar imbalances, not because they're self-destructive but because the nervous system is trying to achieve a different outcome this time. Your body hopes that if you perform compliance perfectly enough in this new context, you'll finally receive the attunement and respect that was withheld before. But the structural conditions make that impossible. You can't heal submission by perfecting submission. ## When "Resistance" Is Intelligence There's a particular phrase that should make your skin prickle: "You're being resistant." It's often delivered with concern, sometimes with gentle exasperation, occasionally with the implication that your resistance is the obstacle to your own healing. But resistance is information. It's your system saying: something here doesn't fit, doesn't feel safe, doesn't honor what I know about myself. Harriet Lerner's work on anger in women's lives makes clear that when we're socialized to prioritize relationships over self-protection, our "resistance" is often the healthiest part of us trying to survive. If you feel resistant to a practitioner's interpretation of your experience, that's not pathology. That's discernment. If you find yourself going silent when they suggest a course of treatment that doesn't land, that silence isn't passive. It's your body refusing to consent to its own erasure. The problem is that many healing modalities were developed within patriarchal, colonial, and capitalist frameworks that position the expert as unquestionable and the sufferer as incompetent to interpret their own experience. When you bring your suffering to these spaces, you're asked to hand over not just your symptoms but your authority over your own bodily knowing. The trade is framed as necessary: trust me completely or you won't get better. But healing that requires you to abandon self-trust isn't healing. It's indoctrination with therapeutic vocabulary. Across the articles we've published on this site, we return again and again to this principle: your body's "no" is sacred data, especially when it arises in response to someone who claims to be helping you. ## The Nervous System Under Observation Picture this: You're lying on a massage table, and the bodyworker finds a knot in your right shoulder. "You're holding so much tension here," they say, pressing deeper. "You need to let this go. Just breathe and release." But the more they press, the more your muscles clench. They interpret this as your resistance to healing. You interpret it as your body screaming that this pressure, this pace, this assumption of access to your tissues without ongoing consent, is not safe. Your dorsal vagal system has gone online. You're immobilized but awake, a state your body knows from every time you had to endure something you couldn't stop. The bodyworker means well. Their training taught them that tension indicates blockage and that skilled manipulation releases it. What their training likely didn't address: how their unexamined authority, their assumption that they know your body's needs better than you do, recreates the very conditions that created the tension in the first place. This is where [THORNE Magnesium Bisglycinate Powder, 60 Servings](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0797HBLL3?tag=spankyspinola-20) can offer support between sessions - not as a fix, but as a way to resource your nervous system when it's metabolizing these complex relational dynamics. Magnesium supports the parasympathetic branch, helping your body shift out of the freeze response when you're away from the triggering environment. But supplementation doesn't resolve the structural issue: that healing spaces often demand a bodily compliance that replicates trauma rather than mending it. Your skin is the boundary between self and world. When someone touches you in a healing context without continually checking in, without honoring your moment-to-moment shifts in comfort and capacity, they're teaching your skin that boundaries are decorative rather than functional. The conscious mind might rationalize: "They're the expert, they know what they're doing." But the skin knows differently. It tightens. It numbs. It protests in the only language it has. ## The Practice of Somatic Consent Here's something you can do in the next five minutes, whether you're currently working with a practitioner or considering it: Sit comfortably and place one hand on your heart, the other on your belly. Think about your most recent healing appointment - therapy, acupuncture, spiritual direction, whatever form it takes. Without trying to change anything, notice: Did your breath deepen or shallow when you walked in? Did your jaw soften or tighten? Where in your body did you feel most yourself, and where did you feel most performed? Now imagine an alternative version of that same appointment where you had permission to say "slow down," "that doesn't fit my experience," or "I need to stop" without risking the relationship or the care you're receiving. Notice what shifts in your body when you imagine that permission. That shift - that subtle opening or exhale - is your system telling you what it needs from healing spaces. You don't need to act on this information immediately. Just acknowledge it. Your body has been speaking this language all along. The practice is learning to listen without judgment, without rushing to fix or dismiss what you find. ## Anger as Relational Data in Healing Contexts When anger surfaces in therapeutic relationships, it's often treated as material to work on rather than information to honor. "Let's explore your anger toward me," the therapist might say, and suddenly your legitimate response to a boundary violation becomes a symptom of your unresolved issues with authority figures. This is a neat trick: it centers the practitioner's comfort while pathologizing your clarity. But what if your anger is the sanest thing in the room? What if it's your body's way of saying: this person just dismissed my lived experience, or this interpretation doesn't match my interior reality, or I'm being rushed toward forgiveness before I've been allowed to fully feel my rage? The fire toolkit we've assembled addresses this precisely: how to tend anger as information rather than evidence of brokenness. When you feel anger toward a healer, that's not transference to be dissolved. It's your system protecting you from repeating an old pattern where someone else's interpretation of your reality mattered more than your own. Some practitioners can hold this. They welcome your anger, your doubt, your disagreement as signs that you're becoming more yourself rather than less. They understand that true healing involves you claiming authority over your own experience, which sometimes means telling them they're wrong. But many practitioners, even skilled and compassionate ones, haven't examined their own attachment to being right, being needed, being the one who knows. [Banyan Botanicals Organic Ashwagandha Tablets](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B000703APK?tag=spankyspinola-20) can support your system's capacity to stay present with difficult emotions rather than collapsing into old appeasement patterns, but the herb can't fix a relational structure that punishes your authenticity. That requires choosing practitioners who view your anger and resistance as intelligence rather than obstacles. ## Recognizing Institutional Mimicry Sometimes the replication of power structures is obvious: the practitioner who talks over you, who ignores your stated needs, who positions themselves as the sole authority on your healing journey. But often it's subtler. It's the practitioner who says all the right words about collaboration and strength but whose body language and session structure tell a different story. Watch for these patterns: Are you allowed to disagree without the session derailing into processing your "difficulty with trust"? Can you question a treatment approach without being labeled resistant? Does the practitioner share their reasoning and invite your input, or do they present conclusions as fait accompli? When you say something doesn't feel right, is that honored as valuable information or treated as a symptom to overcome? Your body will tell you before your mind catches up. That sensation of walking on eggshells, of monitoring your words to avoid the practitioner's disappointment or frustration, of feeling smaller as the session progresses rather than more substantial - these are not signs that you're doing therapy wrong. They're signs that the relational container has drifted into a familiar shape, one where your job is to manage the more powerful person's comfort while your own needs go underground. The herbs we keep in [our herbal cabinet](/herbs) can support nervous system resilience, but they can't replace the need for relationships - including therapeutic ones - where power is acknowledged, negotiated, and shared. Sometimes the most healing choice is to leave a space that asks you to betray your own knowing, even if that space is expensive, well-credentialed, and recommended by people you trust. ## The Myth of Neutrality Many healing modalities claim neutrality: the practitioner is simply a mirror, a container, a facilitator of your own process. But there's no such thing as a neutral relational space. Every interaction carries power dynamics shaped by who has credentials, who's paying whom, who's positioned as knowledgeable and who as seeking, who can end the relationship with minimal consequence and who risks losing access to care. When practitioners claim neutrality, they're often asking you to ignore these realities and focus solely on your internal process. But your internal process is inseparable from the relational field in which it's unfolding. If that field reproduces dynamics of dominance and submission, your nervous system will respond accordingly - not because you're stuck in the past, but because the present actually does contain a threat to your autonomy. This is where [Yogi Tea Stress Relief & Relaxation Variety (5 packs)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0735W1XZP?tag=spankyspinola-20) might offer a small kindness between sessions, a moment of self-tending when you're metabolizing the dissonance between a practitioner's claims of safety and your body's unmistakable signals of danger. But tea, like any herb or supplement, can only support the nervous system processing an experience. It can't make an unsafe relational container safe. True neutrality would require practitioners to continually examine their own power, their own biases, their own investments in being seen as helpful or healing. It would require them to welcome your challenge and dissent as evidence of health rather than pathology. Most practitioners haven't done this work, because most training programs don't teach it. They teach techniques and theories, not the constant vigilance required to notice when you're unconsciously wielding power in harmful ways. ## What Repair Looks Like So what does a healing space look like when it's not replicating old power structures? It starts with practitioners who name the power differential explicitly and invite ongoing negotiation. They say things like: "I'm going to suggest something, and I want you to notice if it fits. If it doesn't, that's important information." They check in frequently, not just about physical comfort but about relational comfort. They welcome your expertise on your own experience as equal to their professional knowledge. These practitioners understand that healing happens in relationship, and that relationship requires both people to be present, responsive, and accountable. They don't hide behind the role of healer or expert. They're willing to be wrong, to adjust, to admit when they've missed something important. They recognize that your resistance might be the healthiest part of you asserting itself. In my own practice, I've learned that the moments when clients push back, disagree, or express anger toward me are often the moments of deepest movement. Not because conflict is inherently healing, but because their willingness to risk the relationship by being authentic signals that their nervous system is starting to trust that this space might actually be different. That their "no" might be honored. That they won't be punished for having needs and limits and a perspective that differs from mine. This kind of relational healing requires practitioners who have done their own work around power, who understand how systems of oppression live in our bodies and shape our interactions, who are committed to something more than symptom reduction. It requires practitioners who see healing not as restoring you to function within unjust systems, but as supporting your capacity to trust yourself, set boundaries, and insist on relationships - including therapeutic ones - that honor your full humanity. ## Questions Readers Often Ask ### How do I tell the difference between productive discomfort and a genuinely harmful dynamic with a practitioner? Productive discomfort usually involves being challenged to expand your capacity or face something you've been avoiding, but you still feel fundamentally respected and seen. Your agency remains intact. Harmful dynamics, by contrast, leave you feeling smaller, more confused about your own perceptions, or walking on eggshells to maintain the practitioner's approval. If you find yourself rehearsing what you'll say to avoid their disappointment, or if you notice symptoms worsening around appointments, your body is giving you clear information. ### Is it okay to confront my therapist or healer about power dynamics, or will that damage the relationship? A practitioner who can't handle being questioned about power dynamics isn't safe enough to help you heal from experiences where your voice was suppressed. A skilled practitioner will welcome this conversation as valuable information about how you're experiencing the relationship. They might feel uncomfortable - that's normal - but they won't make their discomfort your problem to solve. If bringing up a concern derails the entire therapeutic relationship, that tells you something important about whether the relationship could ever have held your full truth. ### What if I can't afford to leave a practitioner who accepts my insurance, even if the dynamic feels off? This is a real constraint, and it's worth acknowledging that economic power shapes access to healing in ways that can trap us in harmful dynamics. In the short term, you can work on building your capacity to hold boundaries within the relationship - saying "that doesn't fit my experience" or "I need to slow down" even when it feels risky. You can also seek supplemental support through peer relationships, sliding-scale practitioners in other modalities, or resources like those gathered at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com). Sometimes healing happens not by finding the perfect practitioner but by learning to trust yourself even in imperfect relational containers. ## Opening Outward The body's protest in healing spaces that replicate power structures isn't a bug in your system. It's a feature, a form of intelligence that deserves respect rather than management. When your shoulders tense at a practitioner's interpretation, when your throat closes around a disagreement, when your gut churns before an appointment, you're receiving information about the relational field that your conscious mind might be working hard to rationalize away. This doesn't mean every moment of discomfort in healing work signals a problem with the practitioner. Growth often involves discomfort. But there's a world of difference between the discomfort of expansion and the discomfort of subordination. Your body knows the difference, even when your mind doesn't want to admit it. The question is whether you'll honor that knowing, whether you'll grant your own physiological wisdom the authority it deserves. What might become possible if you insisted that healing spaces actually be different from the spaces that harmed you? Not just in content but in structure, not just in what's discussed but in how power moves between you and the person claiming to help? This isn't about finding a perfect practitioner - they don't exist. It's about finding practitioners who are willing to stumble toward something better alongside you, who understand that the relationship itself is the medicine, and that medicine only works when it's offered with genuine consent rather than demanded as the price of care. For more explorations of these themes, browse through [more articles in this journal](/articles), where we continue to examine how power, anger, and embodied wisdom intersect in the work of becoming whole. ## Keep reading - [Reclaiming Your Fire: When Anger Becomes Power](/article/reclaiming-your-fire-when-anger-becomes-power) - [Estrangement Rage: When Your Adult Child Goes Silent](/article/estrangement-rage-when-your-adult-child-goes-silent) - [the assessments hub](/assessments) ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 29, 2026. In our work on when healing spaces replicate power structures: the body’s silent protest, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
